ground_truth.title: The Best Sneaker Brands for Every Type of Kicks Fiend
ground_truth.author: Ben Kriz
ground_truth.main_content:
What are the best sneaker brands? The answer is kind of complicated, and the terrain shifts a little every year. The sneaker market is vast and multi-varied, and, at the end of the day, this is all subjective—as any self-respecting film bro will remind you, a billion-dollar Marvel box office haul doesn’t automatically make for great cinema. So instead of rattling off the top brands by revenue or market share (though, yes, some of the biggest names do also make some of the best shoes), let’s approach this with a clear head.

This is a category where three core qualities truly separate the best from the rest: Style, comfort, and materials. Style, because why wear something you don't like the look of? Comfort, because if the shoe isn't supportive, you'll hate it as fast as you loved it. And materials, because you want a sneaker to last you years, not months—or, heaven forbid, weeks.

All of this has been taken into account to create the below: A comprehensive breakdown of what's hot and relevant in today's sneaker market. Below you'll find 18 of the best sneaker brands you need to know—including the requisite Nike, Asics, and New Balance shoutouts—along with four of each brand's best styles right now. From Japanese-made low-tops to Italian-made hikers, it's safe to say one of the 72 pairs below will satisfy your search.

For more intel on kicks we love, check out the GQ Sneaker Shop.

In This Guide

The Heavyweights: Nike, Adidas, New Balance, Converse
The Underdogs: Reebok, Puma, Vans
The Hikers: ROA, Salomon, Merrell, KEEN
The Runners: On, Hoka, Asics, Norda
The Internationals: Mephisto, Moonstar, Reproduction of Found

Nike

Suffice to say, Nike didn't become the biggest, baddest name in the sneaker business by making an inferior product. The Swoosh earned its placed at the top of this list through sheer consistency, building out a deep roster of iconic sneakers that's been hard for any other brand to knock off its perch. After over 50 years in the sneaker game, and with reissues and new styles continuing to hit, Nike is still the one to beat.

Adidas

From extremely popular retro-leaning styles like the Samba and Euro-centric Handball Cup SPZL to forward-looking silhouettes like the Ultraboost, Adidas has worked tirelessly to establish itself as a serious player in the sneaker space, never content to play second fiddle to Nike's daunting presence. Minus one particularly high-profile collaboration, the brand has largely succeeded over the past few years: The Three Stripes has emerged as the preferred collaborator for some of fashion's most interesting minds and wildest dressers, linking and building with the likes of Grace Wales Bonner and Bad Bunny.

New Balance

New Balance has been on a tear for the last few years, and it's not hard to see why. The Boston-based brand has managed to strike a perfect balance between heritage and innovation, churning out some of the most comfortable and stylish sneakers on the market. From the classic 990 series to the more recent 9060, New Balance has something for everyone.

Converse

Converse is the OG of sneaker brands, and for good reason. The Chuck Taylor All Star is one of the most iconic sneakers of all time, and the brand has managed to stay relevant by constantly reinventing itself. From collaborations with high-fashion labels to new takes on classic silhouettes, Converse is a brand that knows how to keep things fresh.

Reebok

Reebok has been making a comeback in recent years, thanks in part to a renewed focus on its heritage styles. The Classic Leather and Club C are both timeless silhouettes that have been updated for a modern audience, while newer models like the Nano X1 are pushing the boundaries of what a training shoe can be.

Puma

Puma has always been a brand that's unafraid to take risks, and that's exactly what has kept it relevant for so long. From the iconic Suede to the more recent RS-X, Puma has a knack for creating sneakers that are both stylish and functional.

Vans

Vans is the brand that defined skate culture, and it's still going strong today. The Old Skool and Sk8-Hi are both classic silhouettes that have been embraced by skaters and non-skaters alike, while newer models like the UltraRange are pushing the boundaries of what a skate shoe can be.

ROA

ROA is a relatively new brand, but it's already made a big impact in the hiking and outdoor space. The Italian brand is known for its technical yet stylish approach to footwear, with models like the Katharina and the Andreas both offering serious performance in a package that looks good on the trail and off.

Salomon

Salomon is a brand that's synonymous with outdoor performance, and its sneakers are no exception. The XT-6 and the ACS Pro are both technical marvels that offer serious support and traction, while still looking sleek enough to wear around town.

Merrell

Merrell is another brand that's known for its outdoor footwear, but its sneakers are just as impressive. The Hydro Moc and the Jungle Moc are both comfortable and stylish, while the Trail Glove is a minimalist shoe that's perfect for hiking or running.

KEEN

KEEN is a brand that's all about adventure, and its sneakers reflect that. The Uneek and the Newport are both unique silhouettes that offer serious performance, while the Targhee is a hiking shoe that's built to last.

On

On is a brand that's all about innovation, and its sneakers are some of the most technologically advanced on the market. The Cloudmonster and the Cloudrunner are both designed for maximum comfort and performance, while the Cloudnova is a lifestyle shoe that doesn't skimp on tech.

Hoka

Hoka is a brand that's known for its maximalist approach to running shoes, but its sneakers are just as impressive. The Clifton and the Bondi are both incredibly comfortable, while the Rincon is a lightweight shoe that's perfect for speedwork.

Asics

Asics is a brand that's been around for decades, and its sneakers are some of the most respected in the running world. The Gel-Kayano and the Gel-Nimbus are both classic silhouettes that offer serious support, while the Gel-Lyte III is a retro runner that's still relevant today.

Norda

Norda is a new brand that's making waves in the trail running space. The 001 is a technical shoe that's built for performance, but it's also stylish enough to wear off the trail.

Mephisto

Mephisto is a brand that's all about comfort, and its sneakers are some of the most comfortable on the market. The Soft-Air and the Match are both designed for all-day wear, while the Step-In is a slip-on that's perfect for travel.

Moonstar

Moonstar is a Japanese brand that's known for its high-quality sneakers. The Shoes Like Pottery line is made using traditional techniques, resulting in a shoe that's both durable and stylish.

Reproduction of Found

Reproduction of Found is a brand that's all about authenticity. The Japanese brand specializes in recreating vintage sneakers using original materials and techniques, resulting in a product that's as close to the original as possible.
with: What are the best sneaker brands? The answer is kind of complicated, and the terrain shifts a little every year.
This is a category where three core qualities truly separate the best from the rest: Style, comfort, and materials.
Suffice to say, Nike didn't become the biggest, baddest name in the sneaker business by making an inferior product.
